passages.explanation: Jesus goes through the grain fields on the Sabbath. His disciples are hungry and begin to pluck heads of grain to eat. The Pharisees object: your disciples are doing what is not lawful on the Sabbath. Jesus responds with David eating the show bread when he was hungry, and with the priests working in the temple on the Sabbath without guilt. Something greater than the temple is here. If you had known what this means — I desire mercy and not sacrifice — you wouldn't have condemned the guiltless. The Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath. Jesus doesn't dismiss the Sabbath — he claims authority over it. The Sabbath was made to serve people, not enslave them.

Jesus enters their synagogue. A man with a withered hand is there. They ask him: is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath? — looking for an accusation. Jesus answers: which of you, if you had one sheep that fell into a pit on the Sabbath, wouldn't grab it and lift it out? How much more valuable is a man than a sheep! So it is lawful to do good on the Sabbath. He tells the man to stretch out his hand. He stretches it out, and it's restored as whole as the other. The Pharisees go out and conspire how to destroy him. They asked a theological question; Jesus gave a humanitarian answer. Their response to a healing is to plot murder — on the Sabbath.

Jesus, aware of their plans, withdraws. Great multitudes follow, and he heals them all. He warns them not to make him known — fulfilling Isaiah's prophecy about the servant: he won't quarrel or cry out. No one will hear his voice in the streets. A bruised reed he won't break. A smoking flax he won't quench, until he leads justice to victory. In his name, the nations will hope. At the height of opposition, Jesus doesn't escalate — he withdraws and serves quietly. Power exercised with restraint.

A demon-possessed man who is blind and mute is brought to Jesus. He heals him so that he speaks and sees. The crowds are amazed: can this be the son of David? The Pharisees counter: this man only casts out demons by Beelzebul, the prince of demons. Jesus dismantles their logic: every kingdom divided against itself is brought to desolation. If Satan casts out Satan, his kingdom can't stand. If I cast out demons by Beelzebul, by whom do your sons cast them out? But if I cast out demons by the Spirit of God, then the Kingdom of God has come upon you. Whoever is not with me is against me. Every sin and blasphemy will be forgiven people, but the blasphemy against the Spirit won't be forgiven — not in this age or the age to come. Blasphemy against the Spirit isn't a one-time mistake — it's looking directly at the work of God and deliberately, persistently calling it demonic. It's the refusal to recognise God when he's standing in front of you.

Either make the tree good and its fruit good, or make the tree corrupt and its fruit corrupt — the tree is known by its fruit. You offspring of vipers, how can you, being evil, speak good things? Out of the abundance of the heart, the mouth speaks. The good man brings out good things from his good treasure; the evil man brings out evil things from his evil treasure. Every idle word that men speak, they'll give account for on the day of judgement. By your words you'll be justified, and by your words you'll be condemned. Words aren't just sounds — they're diagnostic. What comes out of the mouth reveals what's been stored in the heart.

Some scribes and Pharisees ask for a sign. Jesus answers: an evil and adulterous generation seeks a sign, and no sign will be given except the sign of Jonah the prophet. As Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the whale, so the Son of Man will be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth. The men of Nineveh will stand up in the judgement with this generation and condemn it, because they repented at Jonah's preaching — and behold, something greater than Jonah is here. The queen of the south will rise up and condemn this generation, because she came from the ends of the earth to hear Solomon's wisdom — and something greater than Solomon is here. They want a sign on their terms. Jesus offers the ultimate sign — his death and resurrection — but they won't recognise it.

When an unclean spirit goes out of a man, it passes through waterless places seeking rest and finds none. It says: I'll return to my house. When it comes back, it finds the house empty, swept, and put in order. It goes and brings seven other spirits more evil than itself, and they enter and dwell there. The last state of that man becomes worse than the first. So it will be for this evil generation. Moral reform without filling — without a new occupant — leaves a person worse off. An empty house doesn't stay empty.

While Jesus is still speaking, his mother and brothers stand outside wanting to speak with him. Someone tells him: your mother and brothers are standing outside. Jesus stretches out his hand toward his disciples and says: who is my mother, and who are my brothers? Behold, my mother and my brothers! Whoever does the will of my Father in heaven is my brother, sister, and mother. Jesus isn't rejecting his family — he's redefining family. Kingdom loyalty creates a bond deeper than blood.
